summary: Julia Evans discovers the new -y flag in strace after upgrading from Ubuntu 12.04 to 16.04. The flag resolves file descriptor numbers into human-readable filenames in strace output, making it much easier to understand what files a program is reading from and writing to. She demonstrates this by tracing cp and mv commands, showing how cp reads and writes 128KB chunks between files while mv is just a single rename system call. She also highlights the -yy flag which resolves network socket file descriptors into IP addresses and ports.
key_points: The strace -y flag resolves numeric file descriptors into human-readable filenames, eliminating a major usability pain point
Tracing cp reveals it reads and writes 128KB chunks at a time, while mv is just a single rename() system call - explaining the speed difference
The -yy flag goes further by resolving network socket descriptors into source/destination IP addresses and ports
Usability improvements to established developer tools are valuable and worth celebrating
money_quotes: It's important to know about file descriptors, but when I tell people about strace, I always feel like it's kind of annoying! Why can't strace just tell me which file it wrote to, instead of hiding it behind some number?
That's just one system call! No wonder moving is so fast and copying is so slow :)
I love seeing programs like strace get usability improvements!
key_insight: The strace -y flag is a significant usability improvement that replaces opaque file descriptor numbers with human-readable filenames, making system call tracing far more accessible.
